metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `acknowledged` field default (e.g. `false` on first write by S3), define what sets it to `true` (e.g. 'S3 sets `acknowledged: true` only after all label removals in the stack return 2xx, 404, or 409'), and clarify whether S4/S9 consumption changes this field or is entirely separate.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field's lifecycle is ambiguous: the plan states S3 writes it into the outcome record and that 'an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion on restart', but it never specifies (a) what value S3 assigns on first write (`false`? omitted?), (b) what constitutes acknowledgment (label removal fully applied? S4/S9 consumption?), and (c) which component sets it to `true`. An executor must guess whether S3 owns both the write and the flip, or whether S4/S9 flip it after consuming the record.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC5 ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: '`acknowledged` (bool, default `false`): set to `true` by S3 once every label-removal in the stack has completed (2xx/404/409). S4 and S9 read but never write this field; they treat any record regardless of `acknowledged` value as authoritative for the outcome type.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `Autosubmit` label creation (definition in `infra/gerrit/project.config`, ACL grant to the auto-lander service account, and a `setup-project.sh` re-run) as an explicit prerequisite or in-story edit target, since the label does not exist anywhere in the codebase today.
findings.finding: The `Autosubmit` label — the central mechanism S3 removes from changes on failure — does not exist anywhere in the codebase: absent from `infra/gerrit/project.config` (no label definition, no ACL entry), absent from `src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py` (no removal operation), and absent from all of `src/` and `infra/`. S3's core ACs (CI-fail, rebase-conflict, stack hand-back, idempotent ordering) all depend on this label existing and being castable/removable, but no prerequisite story that creates it is enumerated.
findings.location: AC: CI-fail, Rebase-conflict, Stack → owner, Idempotent ordering; infra/gerrit/project.config
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / Edit Targets' section that lists: (1) `infra/gerrit/project.config` — add `[label "Autosubmit"]` block with `function = NoBlock`, `value = +1 Land this change`, `defaultValue = 0`, and an ACL entry granting the auto-lander service account `label-Autosubmit = -1..+1`; (2) `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` — re-run to push the updated config. These are hard prerequisites for every AC in S3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how the loop detects a post-rebase `Verified -1` — explicitly name whether it is event-driven (webhook/Gerrit event stream) or polling, and describe how the loop correlates the vote to the correct (post-rebase) patchset revision rather than a prior one.
findings.finding: The plan specifies WHAT triggers CI-fail detection ('post-rebase Verified -1') but never specifies HOW the loop observes this event. The codebase has a webhook receiver (ADR-0007) and a REST vote path (ADR-0023), but neither the event subscription mechanism nor the correlation of a Verified -1 vote back to the correct patchset/stack is described. Without this, an implementer may build a polling loop (introducing a detection window) or may incorrectly correlate a -1 on the pre-rebase patchset rather than the post-rebase one, leaving the stack in an inconsistent state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet ('on a post-rebase Verified -1, record a ci_failed outcome')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mechanism note: 'The loop subscribes to Gerrit label-vote events (or polls GET /changes/{id}?o=DETAILED_LABELS after each rebase) and checks Verified on the current revision (current_revision field) only — a -1 on any prior patchset is ignored. The current_revision is obtained from the POST /rebase response body.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `stack_id` derivation rule in the Outcome record section (e.g., 'the Gerrit topic name if set, else a hash of sorted change-number list') so S3, S4, and S9 can independently produce the same key.
findings.finding: The `stack_id` field is used as the upsert key throughout the schema and consumed by S4 and S9, but its derivation rule is never defined — no formula (e.g., derived from tip change id, hash of change_ids[], Gerrit topic name) is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Outcome record section: '{stack_id, change_ids[], outcome ∈ {needs_rebase, ci_failed}, failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}' and AC 5 upsert-key clause

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate an `acknowledged` lifecycle protocol with the schema: specify who sets it (S3 after all label removals complete? S4 after consuming the record?), the transition condition, and whether it is S3-only or writable by consumers.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is declared in the record schema and referenced in AC5's restart-recovery rule ('on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion'), but no processing protocol specifies what sets `acknowledged` to true, who sets it, and at what point in the workflow.
findings.location: Outcome record section (field `acknowledged`) and AC5 idempotent-ordering bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Outcome record section: '`acknowledged` is set to `true` by S3 only, after all `Autosubmit` label-removal calls for every `change_ids[]` entry have returned 200, 404, or 409. S4 and S9 treat `acknowledged=false` as "removal may be incomplete" and MUST NOT set this field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Gerrit REST endpoint used to remove `Autosubmit` from each change in the stack (likely `DELETE /a/changes/{id}/reviewers/{account-id}/votes/Autosubmit`), verify it exists on Gerrit 3.14.1, and confirm the auto-lander service account holds the required ACL permission (e.g., `removeReviewer` or equivalent) to remove a vote cast by a different account.
findings.finding: The plan requires removing the `Autosubmit` label from changes whose vote may have been cast by a different account (the agent) than the auto-lander service. The existing `gerrit_client.py` only implements `POST /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` to set its own votes. Removing another account's label vote in Gerrit requires a distinct endpoint (`DELETE /a/changes/{id}/reviewers/{account-id}/votes/{label-id}`) and the `removeReviewer` or `administrateServer` ACL permission — neither the endpoint nor the required permission is named anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail AC and Rebase-conflict AC: 'remove Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack'; Idempotent AC: 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `DELETE /a/changes/{id}/reviewers/{agent-account-id}/votes/Autosubmit` (Gerrit REST `DeleteVote` endpoint, available since Gerrit 2.14). Grant the auto-lander service account `removeReviewer` on `refs/heads/*` in `project.config`. Add this endpoint to `gerrit_client.py` as `delete_label_vote(change_id, account_id, label)` with 404/409 treated as no-op.

findings.finding: The `Autosubmit` label that S3 removes (as its primary failure-response action) is not confirmed to exist in `infra/gerrit/project.config` or anywhere else in the repo. If the label is not provisioned on the live Gerrit 3.14.1 instance, the `POST /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` call with `labels:{Autosubmit:0}` will fail (400 / unrecognized label), making S3's entire CI-fail and rebase-conflict handlers non-functional.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet ('remove `Autosubmit`') and Rebase-conflict bullet ('remove `Autosubmit` and record a `needs_rebase` outcome')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies / Prerequisites' section: 'Requires the `Autosubmit` label to be live in `refs/meta/config` (delivered by [prior story]). S3 cannot be implemented or tested until that label exists on the Gerrit instance.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define who sets `acknowledged` (which story/component, on what trigger) and specify the retirement path for outcome records — either a TTL, explicit deletion by S4/S9 on success, or an archival scheme.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field is present in the outcome record schema but neither who sets it nor when records are deleted/cleaned up is defined. This means records accumulate indefinitely (retirement path is missing) and the 'on restart re-drives unacknowledged record' semantics are ambiguous about what acknowledgement means and who performs it.
findings.location: Outcome record section — '{...acknowledged}'; Idempotent ordering AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit testing specification covering: (a) unit tests for outcome-record upsert idempotency; (b) at least one integration/end-to-end test (or named child ticket) that exercises the 409→record→label-removal sequence against a real or realistic Gerrit boundary; and (c) adversarial tests for mid-sequence failure (e.g., partial label removal + restart) to validate the 'unacknowledged record re-drives removal' invariant.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing failure flows (CI-fail path, 409 rebase-conflict path, idempotent restart/recovery path, stack-atomic Autosubmit removal) but contains zero testing specification — no unit, integration, or end-to-end test strategy is described anywhere. The ACs state desired behavior but do not constitute a test plan. Critically, the sequencing invariant 'outcome recorded BEFORE Autosubmit removal' and the partial-failure scenario (label removal fails mid-stack, then service restarts) have no adversarial test coverage called out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all ACs); 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan with: (1) Unit tests: outcome record upsert-on-same-stack_id, label-removal 404/409 treated as no-op, CI-fail and 409 routing logic. (2) Integration test (Gerrit sandbox or recorded-replay): POST /rebase 409 → outcome written before any label call → all Autosubmit labels removed → restart with unacknowledged record → labels removed again idempotently. (3) Fault-injection test: simulate label-removal failure on change N-of-M mid-stack, assert restart completes remaining removals without duplicating the outcome record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located rule to the schema definition specifying when `failing_change_id` is populated: e.g., 'populated for both outcome types — the change that received Verified -1 (ci_failed) or the change whose POST /rebase returned 409 (needs_rebase); null only if the failing change cannot be determined.'
findings.finding: The `failing_change_id?` field is marked optional in the schema but no decision rule specifies when it is populated versus left null — specifically, whether the `needs_rebase` (409) path always has a failing change id and the `ci_failed` path may or may not.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'failing_change_id?' field

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `tested_sha` in the Outcome record section: specify exactly which SHA is recorded (e.g., 'the Git SHA of the rebased patchset that was submitted to CI') and how S4/S9 are expected to use it.
findings.finding: The `tested_sha` field is included in the record schema but the plan never defines what SHA value it holds (e.g., the patchset SHA before rebase, the SHA after rebase submitted to CI, the current tip SHA) nor how downstream consumers S4/S9 are expected to use it.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'tested_sha' field

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description in the What/Scope section of how restart recovery is triggered (e.g., startup scan of unacknowledged outcome records) so the idempotent-ordering AC criterion has a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.finding: The restart-recovery behavior — 'on restart an unacknowledged record re-drives the label removal to completion' — is specified only in the idempotent AC but has no corresponding description in the plan body (What / Scope sections). This makes it an AC with no described deliverable work, leaving the implementer without guidance on how this recovery is triggered (e.g., startup hook, periodic poll, or re-entry into the main loop).
findings.location: AC: Idempotent ordering under retries/restart (last bullet); What section; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a sentence such as: 'On service startup, the loop scans the state dir for any outcome records where `acknowledged=false` and re-attempts Autosubmit label removal for all change_ids in that record before entering the normal loop.' This makes the recovery mechanism explicit and gives the idempotent AC a concrete deliverable to verify against.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc entry capturing the outcome-record schema, upsert-key semantics, and `acknowledged` field lifecycle (who sets it, when, and what consumers rely on it) so S4 and S9 implementors have a canonical reference.
findings.finding: The outcome-record contract (schema, upsert-key semantics, `acknowledged` lifecycle) is a novel cross-story API consumed by at least S4 and S9, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is planned to capture this architectural decision.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — idempotent ordering bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Create a short ADR (or a section in CLAUDE.md) titled 'Auto-lander outcome record contract' that specifies: (1) the full JSON schema with field semantics, (2) the upsert key (`stack_id`) guarantee, (3) who sets `acknowledged` and when (e.g., S4 on land-command acknowledgment), and (4) the ordering invariant (record written before label removal). Reference this doc from S3, S4, and S9 stories.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: specify that each recorded outcome (ci_failed / needs_rebase) emits a structured log line and at minimum a counter metric; label-removal failures should also be logged at WARN so operators can detect stuck stacks.
findings.finding: No observability specification for the failure-recording path or Gerrit API errors: no logging, metrics, or alerting is mentioned for when a stack is recorded as ci_failed/needs_rebase or when a Gerrit label-removal call fails. Operators have no instrumented signal that the auto-lander is silently failing to clean up stacks.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: 'Each recorded outcome emits a structured log entry (stack_id, outcome, failing_change_id) and increments a named counter metric; transient Gerrit API errors during label removal are logged at WARN level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a retry/backoff policy for all Gerrit API calls (POST /rebase, label SetReview) — e.g., 3 attempts with exponential backoff — and clarify that only HTTP 409 (not 5xx) routes to the needs_rebase path.
findings.finding: No retry/backoff policy is specified for Gerrit API calls (POST /rebase, label-removal SetReview). If Gerrit is transiently unavailable, a single attempt will fail and the loop will either crash or proceed without completing label removal — the restart-recovery path covers the latter but not a crash mid-record-write.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — idempotent ordering bullet; Rebase-conflict AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that outcome record writes are atomic (e.g., write to a tmp file in the same directory then rename) so a crash mid-write leaves the previous valid record intact rather than a corrupt one.
findings.finding: The outcome record file write is not specified as atomic. A crash between opening the file and completing the write could leave a partial/corrupt JSON record, which on restart would be unreadable and might block the re-drive path rather than triggering a clean re-attempt.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'bot writes an append-only JSON record'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to idempotent-ordering AC: 'The outcome file is written via an atomic rename (write to <stack_id>.tmp, fsync, rename to <stack_id>.json) so a mid-write crash leaves the prior record intact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concurrency control for the outcome-record upsert (e.g., advisory file lock, single-writer process guarantee, or atomic rename pattern) to prevent interleaved writes from concurrent loop instances.
findings.finding: The upsert on stack_id is a read-modify-write operation on a shared file, but no concurrency control (file lock, CAS, or single-writer guarantee) is specified. If the auto-lander loop can have overlapping iterations (e.g., a slow previous run still executing while a restart fires), two writers could corrupt the record.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'upsert-keyed on stack_id'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new outcome record store (flat-file JSON, upsert-by-stack_id) intentionally diverges from the existing SQLite-backed DedupStore in `src/rebar/review_bot/dedup.py`, and add a one-sentence rationale in the plan (e.g., 'SQLite not used here because the outcome record must be human-readable by S4/S9 without a DB query' or 'extend DedupStore instead').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new flat-file JSON upsert store (keyed on stack_id, with an 'acknowledged' flag) inside the auto-lander service, while the review-bot already owns DedupStore (src/rebar/review_bot/dedup.py) — an SQLite-backed idempotent-upsert ledger with nearly identical logical semantics (write-on-event, compound-key, idempotent re-run). Without a stated rationale for diverging from the existing SQLite pattern, this risks a NIH parallel implementation of the same upsert-idempotency primitive.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC — Idempotent ordering under retries/restart

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why the outcome record requires a new flat-file store rather than reusing/extending `review_bot/dedup.py`, a Gerrit NoteDb custom note, or another existing persistence mechanism; if a new store is justified, add a brief rationale to the Outcome record section.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel bespoke 'upsert-keyed JSON' outcome store (a flat file in the auto-lander state dir) as a new persistence layer without evaluating existing patterns in the codebase — specifically `review_bot/dedup.py` (a dedup/outcome store) and rebar's own append-only event store — as prior art or alternatives.
findings.location: ## Outcome record

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify atomic write semantics for the JSON outcome record (e.g., write to a sibling `.tmp` file, fsync, then rename into place) so a crash mid-write never corrupts the live record and restart-resumability is guaranteed.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomic write semantics for the JSON outcome record. The AC requires 'outcome is recorded BEFORE Autosubmit removal' for idempotency, but if the process crashes mid-write (e.g., after opening the file but before flushing/closing it), the resulting partial/corrupt JSON leaves the state store in an unreadable state. On restart, the bot cannot distinguish 'record present and valid' from 'record partially written', so the idempotent re-drive guarantee breaks and Autosubmit labels may be left on a failed stack.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' bullet; 'Outcome record' section
findings.suggested_fix: Write the serialized JSON to `<stack_id>.json.tmp`, call fsync/flush, then atomically rename to `<stack_id>.json`. On startup, treat any `.tmp` file whose corresponding `.json` is absent as an incomplete write and discard it (the record is simply absent, triggering a clean re-drive if CI/rebase state is still detectable).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback/back-out specification for the JSON state dir: assert the schema state after rollback (e.g., records persist but are ignored vs. must be purged), include a format-version field so future readers can detect incompatible records, and describe operator action required when rolling back with unacknowledged records present.
findings.finding: The plan provides no back-out path or post-rollback schema-state assertion for the JSON outcome records. The records are described as the 'single source of truth consumed by S4 and S9', but if the bot deployment is rolled back to a prior version that predates S3, those consumers may not exist or may not understand the record format, leaving the state dir in an indeterminate state with no described remediation.
findings.location: 'Outcome record' section; 'Scope' section

findings.finding: The plan specifies semantic error handling (409 → needs_rebase, Verified -1 → remove Autosubmit) but is entirely silent on transient/infrastructure failures: what happens when the Gerrit API call to remove Autosubmit returns 500, times out, or the outcome-event write fails mid-operation? Without retry/backoff or a defined recovery path, a transient failure leaves the stack in an inconsistent intermediate state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail and Rebase-conflict bullets; 'state transitions are idempotent under retries'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'All Gerrit label and rebase API calls use exponential backoff with a cap (e.g., 3 retries, 2-8 s). If removal of Autosubmit from a stack partially fails, the operation is retried from the beginning using the stack's change-list (idempotent label removal is safe to repeat). Outcome-event writes are deduplicated by (change-id, event-type) to tolerate duplicate delivery.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC (e.g. a named integration test, a state-file inspection command, or a Gerrit API query) so that each behavioral claim can produce evidence of satisfaction — including an end-to-end exercise of the 409 and CI-fail paths against a real or realistic Gerrit test instance.
findings.finding: No proving command exists for any of the five ACs. The plan specifies what must happen (record outcome, remove labels, idempotent ordering, no allow_conflicts) but provides zero concrete verification steps — no test invocation, no state-file inspection command, no Gerrit API query to confirm label removal. Every AC is a behavioral claim with no way to produce evidence of satisfaction.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets)
findings.suggested_fix:
For each AC add a 'Verified by:' line, e.g.:
- AC1: `pytest tests/integration/test_failure_handling.py::test_ci_fail_records_outcome -v` asserts state file contains `{outcome: 'ci_failed'}` AND `jq -e '.outcome=="ci_failed"' state/<stack_id>.json`.
- AC2: Same pattern for `needs_rebase`, plus assert the 409 path never sets `allow_conflicts=true` in the rebase request.
- AC5 (idempotency): run the loop twice against the same stack_id and assert `jq '.[] | select(.stack_id==X)' | length` equals 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or document the wipChain / stack-selection interface S3 will consume (e.g., link to the S2 plan's API contract or a shared interface stub) so S3's design is grounded in a confirmed, not assumed, contract.
findings.finding: The Dependencies section asserts 'consumes its wipChain + stack-selection' as if S2's core loop, wipChain contract, and stack-selection are already implemented — but a codebase search finds zero occurrences of 'wipChain', 'wip_chain', 'stack_selection', or 'auto_lander' anywhere in src/ or infra/. This is an unverifiable codebase assertion: S3's design rests on a contract that does not yet exist in code.
findings.location: Dependencies section — 'consumes its wipChain + stack-selection'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (or confirm) that a new Autosubmit-removal helper will be added to gerrit_client.py with explicit 404/409 suppression logic, grounding the AC5 no-op claim in a concrete implementation plan.
findings.finding: AC5 asserts 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op' as an idempotency guarantee, but reading gerrit_client.py in full shows: (a) no method exists for removing or setting the Autosubmit label — only post_vote for LLM-Review exists; (b) the _request() helper raises GerritError on all non-2xx responses with no special 404/409 swallowing. This no-op behavior is neither implemented nor inherited from an existing helper.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (Idempotent ordering under retries/restart): 'label-removal treats 404/409 as a no-op'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Implement remove_autosubmit(change_id) in gerrit_client.py: POST .../review with labels={Autosubmit: 0}; catch GerritError where status in (404, 409) and return without raising.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document which Gerrit REST query option populates owner._account_id on the tip change (e.g., ?o=DETAILED_ACCOUNTS), and confirm whether 'owner' vs. 'uploader' is the correct field for identifying the submitting agent.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'the owning agent is the stack's uploader/owner (owner._account_id on the tip change)' as the mechanism for identifying stack ownership, but a codebase search finds no existing code that fetches owner._account_id from Gerrit's REST API — neither in gerrit_client.py nor anywhere in src/. The field is a Gerrit AccountInfo field that must be explicitly requested; no existing query fetches it.
findings.location: AC3 — Stack → owner: 'owner._account_id on the tip change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Dependencies section (or a new 'Interface Contract' subsection) the concrete shape of `wipChain` and the stack-selection output that S3 will consume from S2 (e.g., module path, data type/schema, and how S3 accesses them), so the S2→S3 boundary is a reviewable contract rather than an implementation detail.
findings.finding: The plan states S3 'consumes `wipChain` + stack-selection' from S2's completed loop, treating these as an already-specified interface contract. However, no definition of `wipChain` (data shape, module location, access method) or the stack-selection algorithm's output interface appears anywhere in the codebase. S3 names these as inputs but does not enumerate where they are defined or what type/shape they expose — leaving the S3 implementer unable to bind to them without reverse-engineering S2's internals.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'this story adds the failure/edge branches to S2's loop and consumes its `wipChain` + stack-selection'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Outcome record section explaining why per-stack upsert JSON in the auto-lander state dir was chosen (e.g. locality with S2's wipChain state, no extra infrastructure, private from developers, and direct file-read access for S4/S9).
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for storing the failure outcome as a per-stack upsert JSON file in the project-infra state dir rather than in an alternative data layer. The 'Outcome record' section describes the schema and upsert semantics in detail but never explains why this storage approach was chosen — omitting the positive rationale required by the G6 rubric.
findings.location: Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Outcome record section: 'This storage layer was chosen for co-location with S2's wipChain state (same process, same state dir), zero additional infrastructure (no DB or message broker), and direct file-read access by S4 and S9 without requiring Gerrit API calls for outcome queries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section (or ADR) explicitly evaluating the Gerrit native `autosubmit` plugin as prior art and documenting why a bespoke auto-lander service is justified over it (or adopt the plugin if it suffices).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke auto-lander service that drives Gerrit's `Autosubmit` label loop (watch, rebase, CI-fail, hand-back) without considering Gerrit's native `autosubmit` plugin, which implements the same pattern as documented prior art from the Android/Chromium lineage.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What / ## Dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Draft a short ADR (e.g., ADR-0042) mirroring ADR-0040's Zuul analysis: list the native plugin's capabilities, identify the specific gap(s) it cannot cover for this project (e.g., custom outcome-record contract for S4/S9 agent consumption, stack-owner semantics), and confirm the bespoke service is the minimal necessary addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (against Gerrit REST API docs or a live probe) the exact HTTP status codes `POST /a/changes/{id}/rebase` returns for: (a) merge conflict, (b) already-at-tip / no-op, (c) permission error; document the verified mapping in an ADR or in the Gerrit client module before coding the 409-routing AC.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `POST /rebase` returns HTTP 409 as the reliable, canonical signal for a rebase conflict, but no Gerrit REST API documentation, ADR, or codebase reference verifies this HTTP status contract — and the existing `gerrit_client.py` has no `POST /rebase` call to learn from.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'Rebase-conflict: on `POST /rebase` `409`...' and 'Never `allow_conflicts` — a `409` from `POST /rebase` routes to the `needs_rebase` path'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `rebase_change(change_id)` method to `gerrit_client.py` with an inline reference to the Gerrit REST API doc section on `/rebase`, and document the status-code contract in a code comment: 409 = conflict (route to needs_rebase), 200 = success, others = propagate as errors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `POST /a/changes/{id}/rebase` returns HTTP 409 (not 400 or another code) specifically for a rebase conflict on Gerrit 3.14.1, add this endpoint to `gerrit_client.py` (or confirm it lives in S2's client), and specify in S3's AC exactly how S3 receives the 409 signal (e.g., exception from the shared client, a structured return value from S2's loop call).
findings.finding: The plan's rebase-conflict AC depends on S2 performing `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` and S3 observing the 409 from that call. This endpoint is entirely absent from the existing `gerrit_client.py` (which lists its endpoints explicitly in its module docstring). S3 must either implement the endpoint itself or trust S2 to propagate the status — but the plan doesn't specify how S3 receives or detects the 409 signal (the existing code has no rebase method to observe).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Rebase-conflict AC: 'on POST /rebase 409, record a needs_rebase outcome'; Dependencies section: 'Depends on S2 (the core loop)'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'whole stack returns to its owning agent' but no agent ownership tracking (mapping from stack/change to owning agent) is found anywhere in the codebase. This is a load-bearing behavioral claim: to return a stack to its owning agent, the system must know which agent owns it. No data structure, field, or protocol for this is specified or probed.
findings.location: Why section: 'the whole stack returns to its owning agent to remediate'; AC bullet 3: 'a failure anywhere in a stack returns the WHOLE stack to its owning agent.'

findings.finding: AC3 says 'a failure anywhere in a stack returns the WHOLE stack to its owning agent' but provides no co-located processing protocol for: (a) how the bot identifies which changes belong to a stack, (b) what concrete operations constitute 'returning the stack' (remove Autosubmit from every change? record one event per change?), and (c) how 'owning agent' is resolved from the change data.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('Never partial-land; never evict a change from within a stack — a failure anywhere in a stack returns the WHOLE stack to its owning agent')

findings.finding: The 'outcome event' recorded on a needs_rebase (AC2) or CI-fail is referenced as a concrete persisted artifact, but the plan never defines its schema: field names, data types, storage location, event type name, or whether it maps to the existing rebar event-log system (which has a strict KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES registry and filename contract).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('record a needs_rebase outcome event-driven on that change only'), AC5 ('the recorded outcome is the authoritative source')

findings.finding: The AC asserts 'state transitions are idempotent under retries' as a present-tense guarantee, but no lander loop implementation exists in the codebase to probe, and the plan provides no mechanism (e.g., idempotency key, compare-and-swap, event-log dedup) by which retries would be made safe. The claim is unbacked.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'state transitions are idempotent under retries.'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that 'remove Autosubmit' treats a Gerrit 404/409 response (label already absent) as a no-op success, and that the outcome record is an upsert keyed on (change_id, failure_type) so re-runs overwrite rather than duplicate.

findings.finding: AC5 claims 'state transitions are idempotent under retries' but provides no processing rule explaining how idempotency is achieved for the outcome-record write — no dedup key, no check-before-write rule, no idempotency token, and no reference to the existing event store's UUID-based append-only idempotency mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 ('state transitions are idempotent under retries')

findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command or verification step. Every criterion is stated as a behavioral requirement with no described test, command, or check that would produce evidence of satisfaction. This means done-ness is unverifiable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all five bullets

findings.finding: AC4 ('Never `allow_conflicts`') is an orphan criterion: no work to prevent `allow_conflicts` usage is described anywhere in Why, What, or Scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'Ensure the rebase call never sets `allow_conflicts=true`; add an assertion/guard in the rebase invocation path that raises immediately if that flag would be passed.'

findings.finding: The CI-fail AC says 'remove Autosubmit, stop driving the stack' but does not specify the scope: does Autosubmit get removed from only the failing change or from every change in the entire stack? The 'never partial-land' AC implies the whole stack must be returned, yet the CI-fail AC's wording could be read as acting on one change only, leaving siblings with Autosubmit still set and free to land.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the CI-fail AC to read: 'on a post-rebase `Verified -1`, remove `Autosubmit` from **all changes in the affected stack** and stop driving the stack; post NO bot comment.'

findings.finding: The compound failure-response (remove `Autosubmit` label AND record `needs_rebase` outcome event) is two sequential external mutations with no specified execution order or atomicity boundary. The plan asserts 'state transitions are idempotent under retries' but does not design the ordering or recovery path. A crash between the two operations leaves the stack in an ambiguous state — either label removed but no outcome recorded (agent never sees the signal; stack silently orphaned), or outcome recorded but label still present (bot may re-drive a failed stack). Both violate the plan's core guarantee that 'the whole stack returns to its owning agent.'
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Rebase-conflict' bullet: 'remove `Autosubmit` and record a `needs_rebase` outcome'; 'state transitions are idempotent under retries' clause
findings.suggested_fix: Specify write-first ordering: record the outcome event (needs_rebase or ci_failed) BEFORE removing the Autosubmit label. The outcome record is the durable checkpoint; label removal is a safe idempotent follow-on. On restart, the loop checks for an unacknowledged outcome record and re-drives the label removal. Add this ordering to the AC so the implementation is unambiguous.

findings.finding: The compound failure-response operation — (1) remove `Autosubmit` label AND (2) record the `needs_rebase` outcome — is not specified to be atomic. If the process crashes or the second call fails after the first succeeds, the system is left in an inconsistent state: label removed but no outcome recorded (or vice versa), which orphans the stack silently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Rebase-conflict' bullet and 'state transitions are idempotent under retries' clause
findings.suggested_fix: Define a write-first ordering: record the `needs_rebase` outcome event BEFORE removing the Autosubmit label. Make the outcome record the durable checkpoint; the label removal is then a safe idempotent follow-on (can be retried on restart). Document this ordering in the AC so the implementation is unambiguous.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
